Thank you, Mr. President, distinguished delegates. I am extremely concerned about the human rights situation in South Sudan. This is one of the world's forgotten crisis. We are at a dangerous point when rising violence is combined with deepening uncertainty over South Sudan's political trajectory as the peace agreement comes under severe strain. In the past three months, both both government and opposition forces and their allied militias have launched attacks on residential areas in Upper Nile, Chonglay, Unity, Central Equatoria, Western Bar, Al Gazal, Western Ecuadoria, and Warup states. In northern Chunglay, more than 280,000 people fled as their homes, schools, and healthc care facilities came under fire. January saw a further deterioration. My office documented that 189 civilians were killed and many more injured as human rights violations and abuses surged by 45% compared with December. Civilians are bearing the brunt of a spike in indiscriminate attacks including aerial bombardments, deliberate killings, abductions and conflict related sexual violence. These attacks raise serious concerns under international law. I was horrified to learn about an attack earlier this week by government forces and allied militia on a village in Ayod County, Chonglay state. Witnesses told my colleagues that troops ordered residents to gather and then opened fire on the ground, killing 21 unarmed civilians, including women and children. Military discipline appears to have collapsed in both government and opposition forces in Chonglay and eastern Equatoria where troops have demonstrated a near total disregard for civilian protection. The recent escalation comes against the backdrop of rising tensions and civilian deaths throughout 2025. My office recorded an increase of 40% in the number of people killed and injured last year to more than 5,100 compared with 2024. Tragically, two UN staff members were among those killed, which is completely unacceptable. Our team has monitored more 250 individual cases of women and girls who suffered conflict related sexual violence. The true figures are likely much higher. We also documented a disturbing increase to 550 in the number of civilians abducted by opposition forces and their allies in 2025. Distinguished delegates, rising tensions are characterized by hate speech and incitement to violence targeting entire communities and ethnic groups. In one audio recording authenticated by the UN mission, a senior military official urged his forces to spare no lives and to destroy civilian homes, livestock, and property. Let me be clear, acts of hate speech and incitement to violence may amount to international crimes. In the context of armed conflict, those risks become particularly acute. Those responsible for such crimes may be prosecuted under international law. My office has also documented increased threats to civic space. The National Security Service senses the media, monitors civil society activities, intimidates journalists and political activists, and targets human rights defenders and activists who speak out. We documented 27 incidents of intimidation and arbitrary arrest of journalists, civil society actors, and human rights defenders throughout the year, affecting 62 people, including 28 women. Some were held for weeks before being granted bail. Others were released without charge within hours. Open civic space is essential to inclusive constitution building and transitional justice and for free, fair, credible, and inclusive elections to be held as scheduled in December 2026. Distinguished delegates, sustainable peace cannot be achieved without accountability for serious violations and abuses. That accountability must be owned and driven by national authorities in order to restore confidence in institutions, affirm the rights of victims, and prevent cycles of repeated violence. International efforts cannot replace the primary responsibility of the state. The government has taken some important steps towards implementing its commitments, including the adoption of laws, establishing the Commission for Truth, Reconciliation, and Healing, and the Compensation, and Reparation Authority. The task ahead is to translate these frameworks into fully functioning institutions that deliver truth, justice, and reparations for victims and survivors. Moving forward with the establishment of the hybrid court for South Sudan will be essential to ensure accountability and redress to victims. This is long overdue. Mr. President, the dire humanitarian situation in South Sudan and the war across the border are intrinsically linked to rising insecurity and wider human rights violations and abuses. More than 10 million people require humanitarian assistance, including 7.5 million facing food insecurity. The arrival of more than 1.3 million refugees and returnees from Sudan, and the disruption of trade puts even more pressure on very limited resources. But South Sudan is extremely dangerous for humanitarian workers. There were 350 attacks on staff and facilities in 2025, up by more than third from the previous year. The humanitarian crisis will not be solved without addressing insecurity and weak governance. And I urge the government the government to remove physical and bureaucratic obstacles to access and to invest in resilience and recovery. Mr. President, human rights monitoring provides a warning signal. That system is flashing red for South Sudan. We need urgent action to preserve the peace agreement and prevent fragmentation and cycles of retaliation that could herald a return to allout civil war. I call for an immediate halt to all hostilities and reiterate the need for all parties to the conflict to abide by international human rights and international humanitarian law. I urge them to recommmit to inclusive dialogue and implement the peace agreement. I call for accountability for serious violations of international humanitarian and human rights law, including conflict related sexual violence. And I urge political and military leaders to reject ethnic incitement, end the mobilization of communities along ethnic lines, and uphold their obligation to prevent ethnic-based violence. The situation in South Sudan demands the full attention of the international community, sustained monitoring and robust advocacy to ensure that the rights, dignity, and safety of civilians are at the center of national and international approaches. I reiterate my call on the international community to intensify its engagement and to support the people of South Sudan in their efforts towards healing and long-term stability. Thank you.
